metadata.seed: I work and work and work but I can't seem to get ahead. The volume at work has gotten so bad that I can't keep up. I'm getting injured and no one cares. I can barely pay my bills and it feels like there is no end in sight.

patient.query: I work and work and work but I can't seem to get ahead. The volume at work has gotten so bad that I can't keep up. I'm getting injured and no one cares. I can barely pay my bills and it feels like there is no end in sight.

automatic_thoughts: there is no end in sight

neutral: Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds incredibly overwhelming to be facing so much – the high workload, injuries, financial strain, and feeling like no one notices. Imagine a friend were in your shoes, what might they say about your situation and what strengths would they recognize you using to cope?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like you're carrying a tremendous weight right now, and it's understandable that you'd feel overwhelmed when facing so many challenges. It takes a lot of strength to keep going when things feel this difficult.

patient.query: I guess my friend might say something like, “You’re really doing your best, even when it feels like everything’s piling up.” They’d probably see that I keep pushing through, even when it feels hopeless, like I’m just trying to keep my head above water. But honestly, it’s hard to see those strengths in myself right now. I just feel so drained and overlooked.

behaviors: trying to keep my head above water

patient.query: I don’t know... I guess they might say I'm good at connecting with people or understanding their problems. Sometimes, I can really empathize with others, even when I'm feeling lost myself. But when it comes to my own situation, I just feel so stuck that it’s hard to see any strengths at all. It’s like I’m in a fog, and I’m not sure how to clear it.

automatic_thoughts: I can't see a way out

patient.query: Yeah, I guess it’s just... when I’m feeling overwhelmed, I get so caught up in what’s not working. I start to focus on everything I’m failing at. It feels like this cloud that just hangs over me, and it drowns out any sense of what I might be doing well. It’s like I’m so busy trying to keep up that I can’t step back and see the bigger picture.

automatic_thoughts: I can’t see the bigger picture

They might notice the small victories that I just brush aside. But when I’m in the thick of all this, I can’t help but feel like those little things don’t really count for much. It’s hard to appreciate anything when everything feels so heavy.

automatic_thoughts: those little things don’t really count for much
I can’t help but feel like I’m not doing enough

patient.query: I guess it’s like... I have this idea in my head that if I’m not making big strides, then it doesn’t matter. I put so much pressure on myself to achieve these huge goals, and anything less just feels pointless. It’s like my brain is stuck in this loop of “not enough,” and those small wins don’t seem to break through that. It’s frustrating because I want to feel proud, but it just feels overshadowed by all the things I haven't done.

automatic_thoughts: if I’m not making big strides, then it doesn’t matter
my brain is stuck in this loop of 'not enough'
small wins don’t seem to break through

patient.query: I think it’s probably been there for a long time. Growing up, I just felt like I had to prove myself, you know? It was always about being the best, whether in school or sports—nothing less was acceptable. I guess, over the years, when I didn’t meet those expectations, it just kind of stuck with me. Now, it feels like every step I take has to be a giant leap, or else I’m falling behind. It’s exhausting to constantly chase that

automatic_thoughts: every step has to be a giant leap

patient.query: Yeah, I think part of it came from my family. There was this unspoken rule that you had to succeed, otherwise you didn’t really matter, you know? My parents always celebrated achievements, but when I struggled or didn’t live up to that image, it felt like I was disappointing everyone. It made me feel like if I wasn’t the best, I was somehow failing—not just at my goals but at being a person. That pressure has just woven itself into how I see everything now
